challenges.stem: A job candidate arrives at an interview wearing an expensive watch, casually mentions their Ivy League degree, and name-drops a famous former boss — all in the first two minutes. What information should you extract from this behavior?

options.text: Their status is uncertain and they're investing costly resources to establish it — which means they may or may not be as qualified as the signals suggest
They're clearly high-status and qualified since they can afford these signals
They're insecure and compensating — strong candidates don't need to signal
It's irrelevant social behavior and you should focus only on their technical answers

challenges.explanation: Aggressive signaling tells you status is 'in play' — uncertain and needing proof. This is Bayesian evidence that updates in both directions: they might genuinely be accomplished (the signal is honest) or they might be stretching (the signal is inflation). It's not automatically positive (option B) or negative (option C). And it's definitely not irrelevant (option D) — signaling behavior IS information about status confidence.

challenges.stem: Mark Zuckerberg wore a hoodie to Wall Street investor meetings in 2010. Analysts were scandalized, but his status only increased. What force made this countersignal work?

options.text: Power — his status was so established (worth $10B) that NOT signaling communicated 'I don't need your approval,' which only the very powerful can afford
Identity — the hoodie was authentic to his identity as a tech founder and signaled he wouldn't change for anyone
Status — wearing informal clothes in a formal setting disrupted the hierarchy and put him on top
Trust — by being informal he signaled trustworthiness and accessibility that suited executives lack

challenges.explanation: Countersignaling works through power: only someone whose position is unquestionable can afford to not play the signaling game. The room already knew Zuckerberg was worth billions — the hoodie said 'I'm beyond needing to impress you.' If an unknown 26-year-old did the same thing, it would read as incompetence, not power. The mechanism is power-based, not identity or trust.

pairs.back: A signal is honest BECAUSE it's costly — only genuinely fit organisms can survive despite the handicap. The peacock's tail proves fitness precisely because it's a burden.
Education works as a signal not because of knowledge gained, but because completing it demonstrates underlying qualities (intelligence, persistence) that employers can't observe directly.
NOT signaling when signaling is expected — communicates status so secure it doesn't require proof. Only works when the audience already knows your position.
Low status: can't afford to signal. Middle status: signals aggressively (proving position). High status: countersignals (so established that trying looks weak).

pairs.left: MBA from a top school ($200k + 2 years)
pairs.right: Costly signal of discipline, class, and network — works because it's expensive to fake

pairs.left: CEO wearing sneakers to a formal board meeting
pairs.right: Countersignal — status so established that informality reads as confidence, not ignorance

pairs.left: Gazelle bouncing high in front of a predator
pairs.right: Honest signal: 'I'm so fit I can waste energy — don't bother chasing me'

pairs.left: Posting a photo from a first-class lounge once
pairs.right: Cheap/questionable signal — low cost reduces informational value (could be upgrade or one-time)

pairs.left: Billionaire moving to 'dark social' (no public presence)
pairs.right: Privacy as the new countersignal — absence from public platforms signals 'beyond needing attention'

before.label: Reading signals at face value
before.text: Impressive credentials and expensive displays mean someone is impressive. Casual appearance means someone isn't serious. I judge people by what they show me.

after.label: Reading the signal economics
after.text: I ask: what's the cost of this signal? Who can afford it? What audience is it designed for? Aggressive signaling means status is uncertain. Countersignaling means status is unquestionable. Both are data — but about confidence level, not actual quality.
